item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this one and was left wondering what, exactly, they are trying to build.

item.title: Rewrite request
item.content:encoded: Hey author, I had some ideas for this article that (I think) are pretty cool. I posted the idea in the Ideas and Brainstorming forum and someone told me it was similar to this SCP and might work better as a rewrite of yours. If you would rather keep this work as it is, that's completely understandable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good concept, I personally find it interesting that they keep making headache devices for some reason. A little hint on why would help significantly.

Last log in the first list of testing procedures has a brief mentioning of "SCP-XXX" in it.
The description of the reassembly process is incorrect from my view: You have described it as though the rats reassemble the device identically to how it was before, or as close as they can. The testing logs contradict this, since it seems extremely rare for them to replicate the original condition of the device, either repairing it to a functional level when it previously was broken, or augmenting the supplied parts into a device that causes nausea and headaches.

Still, I'm interested, so i'll give it my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You should probably edit the main list with the nickname for this SCP- it still says [ACCESS DENIED].

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I were gonna make a guess, I'd say they're completely sapient, and they're only building tinnitus bots in an attempt to escape their hominid captors. Once they make their escape, they'll build a city, Rats of Nimh style. Or, that's what I'd guess the author was shooting for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really like that the tests went in a direction other than "when you mix the parts of two devices, you get a result that is a mix of the two devices!".

Makes me wonder what those rats are up to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My personal headcanon is that these are somehow related to SCP-039- either prototypes or an attempt at a reverse engineered 2.0 version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it makes you feel better about it, that idea honestly never even occurred to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only issue with this article is why no one in Foundation Research would think to take the sound that is being generated and step the frequencies down to human hearing range? It's obvious that it is tuned to rat hearing, outside the range of human hearing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me of SCP-039 quite a lot, at least at the beginning.

I'll need to reread it again, but as it is I'm not quite sure if this SCP is different enough from 039 in term of core idea to sustain itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea has some potential however in its current form it has some problems.

1) There is a lot of extraneous detail. You could convey the same information in much shorter article.

2) Some hint of why the rats are this way/what their purpose is would be good.

3) The note at the end telling foundation employees to not let the builder rats have access to mechanical devices comes across as rather silly. (And implies a level of stupidity on the behalf of foundation personal which is generally frowned upon in current writing).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a few bits here and there that stand out. 'Terms like 'rat chow' are too casual, try using terms like 'nutritional supplement' or even just 'any commercially available rodent feed.'

A hint as to the ultimate purpose of what they build when given random parts would be nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with the others that there's some real potential here, but it needs some heavy polishing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From the article,

Instances of SCP-2114 appear physically identical to the common white laboratory rat

I would say that SCP-2114 and non-anomalous rats are physically identical. I would suspect that they look like a common lab rat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And you have given me an odd headcanon that these rats are attempting to establish communications via rebuilding devices.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll tell you what intrigued me: the anomalous radio activity and auditory hallucinations at the beginning. Particularly the radio activity, the idea of Radio Rats really drew me in, but there was so little pay off for it. It took way too long to get back to that; before getting to that I was just picturing the Cinderella rats but with gears instead of chiffon.

Upvoted because I see some real potential here.

(Also, lolFoundation should probably be cut. This includes the note at the end of the first computer test and the addendum.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with MrWrong. It is a good concept, and intriguing. An idea would be to add a picture, just to show how similar they are to normal rats.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think sex segregation needs to be mentioned at all. It's kinda lab protocol (I think) to do that in the first place. Something that most researchers would be familiar with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is workable, but it feels lacking at this point. Sure, we got these rats that can dismantle and put together mechanical items. But why do they do so? Given they originate from a lab, maybe we can tie that aspect in?

The ending is somewhat anti-climatic, what with the rats building a device that causes headaches… and then there's a blackboxed unknown SCP that was agitated because of that… It didn't really add much to any mystery etc. behind these rats and their actions.

I also agree that sex segregation for the rats should be part of the containment procedures.

item.title: I like the idea, but...
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but after reading it, I was left with a big, "So what?"

There doesn't seem to be a thread of mystery tying the whole thing together. It's like you showed your entire hand in the writing of the article, and it didn't leave me wanting more.

Also, a handful of containment procedures (like keeping males and females separate to prevent breeding) seemed to slither its way from the containment procedures section into the description.
